topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy dropping onto a desk. My fingers hovered over the screen, trembling slightly before I clicked it open. The words swam before my eyes: "We regret to inform you that your application for the [Scholarship Name] has not been successful at this time." A strange, cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward until my chest felt constricted. I reread the rejection letter three times, searching for some hidden flaw, some tiny oversight I had missed. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small room, my footsteps echoing hollowly against the quiet walls. The air suddenly felt thin, suffocating. When my roommate walked in, I didn't look up, just mumbled something about needing air, my voice tight and strained. The silence that followed was thick with unspoken disappointment.

[story 2]

I stared at the rejection notice, the glossy paper feeling like a physical affront. It was supposed to be the key, the validation of years of grueling work, and now it was just… dismissal. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks a deep crimson. I picked up the letter, the edges slightly crinkled where my thumb pressed too hard. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air. I walked over to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to let in a sliver of harsh afternoon light. The glare felt accusatory. Every carefully constructed sentence in my application, every late night spent perfecting an essay, suddenly felt cheapened, rendered meaningless by this sterile notification. I turned back, my hands balled into fists at my sides. I didn't speak to anyone; I simply walked away, the sound of my own heavy breathing the only noise in the hallway.

[story 3]

The screen glowed with the unforgiving verdict. "Application Denied." The words were stark, brutal, and delivered with an impersonal finality that scraped against my nerves. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. A wave of intense frustration washed over me, making my vision swim slightly. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound sharp enough to startle the quiet apartment. My pulse hammered against my temples. I began to pace the length of the living room, running my hands through my hair repeatedly, trying to physically shake off the heavy sense of being overlooked. Why? What had I done wrong? The question echoed in the empty space where confidence used to reside. I stopped abruptly, staring at my reflection in the dark monitor—a stranger with hollow eyes, utterly deflated.
